scenario: A food distributor's demand forecasting model is 15% less accurate than the Excel model it replaced, despite using more data.
why: More data isn't better data. The model consumes inconsistent or stale data from poorly integrated sources. The model isn't wrong; the data pipeline is.

scenario: A utility company wants to offer real-time energy usage insights to customers, but their metering data arrives in 24-hour batches.
why: You can't build real-time features on batch data. No UX or model compensates for data that's a day old. Infrastructure needs to change first.

scenario: Two departments in a government agency report different counts for the same metric to parliament because they query different databases.
why: No single source of truth. Two systems, two versions of reality. Data architecture and integration problem.

scenario: A pharmaceutical company's clinical trial data takes 6 weeks to compile for regulatory submission because it sits in 8 separate systems with different schemas.
why: Data spread across incompatible systems with no unified model. The delay is manual stitching. Fix the data architecture.

scenario: An e-commerce platform's recommendation engine gives great suggestions on the website but terrible ones in the mobile app, using the same model.
why: Same model, different results means different input data. The mobile app sends incomplete user behavior data. The model is fine; the mobile data pipeline is broken.

scenario: A logistics company built a route optimization tool, but dispatchers can only use it for 30% of routes because partner carriers don't share data.
why: Optimization works. Partner carrier data is inaccessible. Integration gap. The tool is limited by architecture, not by its algorithm.

scenario: Every time the ERP vendor releases a quarterly update, two of your custom connections break and take a week to fix.
why: Fragile connections without contract testing or abstraction layers. Integration architecture and resilience problem.

scenario: You want to build a smart document search across your company's SharePoint, but employees have been saving files with names like 'final_v3_REAL_final_updated.docx' for 10 years.
why: Instinct says smart search handles messy naming. But searching over chaos gives confidently wrong results. Document governance and metadata structure need fixing first.

scenario: A retail chain uses personalized marketing emails. Open rates are high, but conversion is 0.3% because recommended products are often out of stock.
why: Personalization works (high open rates prove it). Inventory data is stale or disconnected from the marketing system. The recommendations are for products that don't exist on the shelf.

scenario: Your HR department wants to screen CVs automatically. Your historical hiring data shows you've only ever hired from 4 universities.
why: Training data reflects a biased historical process. Automating on this data amplifies bias. Fix the data and process before automating.

scenario: A city's parking app shows real-time availability, but drivers report that 'available' spots are always taken when they arrive.
why: Sensor data latency or coverage gaps mean the app's data is wrong. The UI faithfully displays bad data. Fix the data pipeline.
